    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

A second, denser table to stress column measuring:

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

alphabetagamma
123middletrailing
 
fn generated(value: usize) -> usize { value + 1 }

Generated Section 212

franken_markdown

A clean-room, zero-dependency Rust engine that turns Markdown into a gorgeous self-contained HTML page and a professional-typography PDF — with embedded subset fonts, real kerning, ligatures, and LaTeX-grade line breaking. This document is the visual gauntlet: if it renders beautifully, the engine works.

Inline formatting

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

A second, denser table to stress column measuring:

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

alphabetagamma
123middletrailing
 
fn generated(value: usize) -> usize { value + 1 }

# Render this document to a PDF

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

alphabetagamma
123middletrailing
 
fn generated(value: usize) -> usize { value + 1 }

Generated Section 229

franken_markdown

A clean-room, zero-dependency Rust engine that turns Markdown into a gorgeous self-contained HTML page and a professional-typography PDF — with embedded subset fonts, real kerning, ligatures, and LaTeX-grade line breaking. This document is the visual gauntlet: if it renders beautifully, the engine works.

Inline formatting

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

A second, denser table to stress column measuring:

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

alphabetagamma
123middletrailing
 
fn generated(value: usize) -> usize { value + 1 }

Generated Section 238

franken_markdown

A clean-room, zero-dependency Rust engine that turns Markdown into a gorgeous self-contained HTML page and a professional-typography PDF — with embedded subset fonts, real kerning, ligatures, and LaTeX-grade line breaking. This document is the visual gauntlet: if it renders beautifully, the engine works.

Inline formatting

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

A second, denser table to stress column measuring:

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

alphabetagamma
123middletrailing
 
fn generated(value: usize) -> usize { value + 1 }

Generated Section 247

franken_markdown

A clean-room, zero-dependency Rust engine that turns Markdown into a gorgeous self-contained HTML page and a professional-typography PDF — with embedded subset fonts, real kerning, ligatures, and LaTeX-grade line breaking. This document is the visual gauntlet: if it renders beautifully, the engine works.

Inline formatting

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

A second, denser table to stress column measuring:

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

alphabetagamma
123middletrailing
 
fn generated(value: usize) -> usize { value + 1 }

Generated Section 256

franken_markdown

A clean-room, zero-dependency Rust engine that turns Markdown into a gorgeous self-contained HTML page and a professional-typography PDF — with embedded subset fonts, real kerning, ligatures, and LaTeX-grade line breaking. This document is the visual gauntlet: if it renders beautifully, the engine works.

Inline formatting

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

A second, denser table to stress column measuring:

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.

alphabetagamma
123middletrailing
 
fn generated(value: usize) -> usize { value + 1 }

Generated Section 273

franken_markdown

A clean-room, zero-dependency Rust engine that turns Markdown into a gorgeous self-contained HTML page and a professional-typography PDF — with embedded subset fonts, real kerning, ligatures, and LaTeX-grade line breaking. This document is the visual gauntlet: if it renders beautifully, the engine works.

Inline formatting

You get bold, italic, bold italic, inline code, and strikethrough — composably, so you can write bold-italic code or a bold link to the docs inside a sentence. Emphasis nests correctly: bold with italic inside, italic with bold inside, and the tricky bold italic closing run all resolve the way CommonMark says.

Links come in three flavors — an inline link, a reference link, and a bare autolink like https://rust-lang.org or even https://example.com/path?q=1 in running text. Hard breaks work too — this line ends with two spaces and wraps to a new line.

Lists, nested and mixed

Unordered lists nest cleanly:

  • Top-level bullet with a fair amount of text so the line wraps and the Knuth–Plass breaker has a real paragraph to optimize across the measure.
    • A nested bullet
      • A doubly-nested bullet
    • Back to one level of nesting
  • Another top-level item

Ordered lists keep their counters and nest too:

  1. First step
  2. Second step
    1. A sub-step
    2. Another sub-step
  3. Third step

Task lists track state:

  • Parse the document into an AST
  • Shape glyphs with kerning and ligatures
  • Knuth–Plass line breaking
  • Liang hyphenation

Tables

GFM pipe tables become measured-column grids with per-cell alignment:

FeatureStatusNotes
HTML outputworkingall-in-one, themeable
PDF outputworkingembedded fonts, kerning, KP
Syntax highlightworkinga dozen-plus languages
Zero dependenciesyesthe engine has no crates

A second, denser table to stress column measuring:

LangKindHighlighted
Rustsystemsyes
Pythonscriptingyes
JSONdatayes
TOMLconfigyes

Blockquotes

Blockquotes get a soft accent bar and a tinted background, and they wrap across multiple lines just like a real preview pane would render them.

Nested quotes indent further, so threaded commentary stays readable even a couple of levels deep.

Code blocks

Fenced code blocks sit on a rounded panel with clean-room syntax highlighting for the languages that actually show up in technical writing.

fn fibonacci(n: u32) -> u64 {
    let (mut a, mut b) = (0u64, 1u64);
    for _ in 0..n {
        (a, b) = (b, a + b); // tuple-destructuring update
    }
    a // the nth Fibonacci number
}

def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:
    a, b = 0, 1
    for _ in range(n):
        a, b = b, a + b  # parallel assignment
    return a

{
  "engine": "franken_markdown",
  "deps": 0,
  "outputs": ["html", "pdf"],
  "fast": true
}

# Render this document to a PDF
fmd render examples/showcase.md --to pdf --out showcase.pdf

That's the tour. Everything above is rendered by hand-written, dependency-free Rust — the parser, the syntax highlighter, the font subsetter, and the PDF writer are all our own code.
